channel.title: Comments for page "Your Last First Day"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Your Last First Day"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So is 4987 dead? I’m guessing 3125 ate it, already?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha! Nice. Agreed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This whole series is amazing and I'm enjoying it and (the sequel series) utterly. I'm mostly commenting to register the completely inconsequential opinion that the worms should be named "mematodes."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was wondering when someone was going to notice that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so I'm like almost four years (?!) late on this, but I just woke up and suddenly wondered: why doesn't Marion finally see SCP-4987 properly when she takes the Class-Z? It's previously established that it's antimemetically cloaked and follows her around everywhere… shouldn't it be in the elevator with her as the dose takes effect?

Is it actually physically invisible in addition to being antimemetic?

Does it pick up information about SCP-3125 and then get eaten by it?

Does it realize that Marion taking the Class-Z means that it can never eat her memories again, and decide to abandon her?

I don't know, it just seemed odd to have this beastie mentioned so often in the previous chapters, including the one right before this, only to have it vanish without a whisper here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
very much enjoyed the story, thought it was well told and the arc of the character is interesting. I'm not as large a fan of the ending as a lot of the other commenters seem to be, as it has a bit too much of a "it was all for naught, the bad guy wins, and the Foundation is shortly doomed" vibe to make it satisfying. The fact that the vault was empty, and no works had been completed felt… odd… to me.

My head cannon, possibly to allow me to feel a bit more positive about the ending, is that the bomb took a bite out of 3125, slowing it down enough for another attempt at stopping it.

Edit: Just found there are more stories, Please ignore the above until i read the rest of them! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Said this elsewhere, saying this again.

Case colourless green and this are the reason i made an account. Not upvoting it would be criminal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That makes sense, even if she was destroyed by the bomb, she was already on z class mnestics…
But if I remember well, it does not matter if you Remember yourself, what really matters is if someone under the effect of mnestics remembers you. which the O5 might do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
all of it should be present in any physical space with which it interacts, and so it was also destroyed conceptually and its effect erased from existence.

It exists in an alien noosphere beyond our comprehension. It also exists within the Fifth Church.

Unless there's a good reason this couldn't be true, it's now my headcanon.

"Five-Five-Five-Five-Five" will most likely be that reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It really seems to me as though the weapon should have destroyed 3125, since the barrier was no longer in place and it was controlling the people it had taken, so it, or a part of it, was within range, but proximity with regards to 3125 is mental, and since it is a single mental construct, all of it should be present in any physical space with which it interacts, and so it was also destroyed conceptually and its effect erased from existence. Unless there's a good reason this couldn't be true, it's now my headcanon. Even if it wasn't that stroke of brilliance on Marion's part, though, opening up to the threat to destroy it with one last sacrifice, this was a brilliant tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go for pharmacological trepanation and take both!

item.title: Re: So Yeah
item.content:encoded: You were fast enough! Congratulations.

item.title: Re: So Yeah
item.content:encoded:
Basically, yes. Staff is not reserving 3125 for anyone, even qntm. If someone else were to come along and place something there, it would have to sink or swim like any other article.

Really, the only thing that keeps 3125 reserved is if the readership at large wants to see qntm's 3125 there, it'll be hard to make another article stick. But that's the membership speaking, not staff.

item.title: Re: So Yeah
item.content:encoded: I don't think there is any process other than the administration's good graces which prevents somebody filling SCP-3125 right now, but I have a draft in progress right now (code is 55555) which is about 95% complete.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
(My intention is only to put it there as a placeholder (in case qntm wants to write a better version) so other people won't use it. 3125 is a very nice round number after all…)

Except for cases like the 2000 and 3000 contest participants, we do not allow authors to reserve slots.

if you hate me for doing this or what I'm doing is against site policy, comment on the 3125 page and depending on your argument I'll probably delete it.

If you reserve a slot without permission and there is no material in said slot, that counts as unfinished work and is grounds for summary deletion, which staff can and will carry out without needing to comment on the page directly.

item.title: Re: So Yeah
item.content:encoded: qntm is already doing that.

item.title: Just ignore me, idiot coming through.
item.content:encoded: stupid stupid stupid

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had assumed she was talking to a representative, and that representative was herself. After all, the ideas were in her head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The not-people who were not-going to not-do things to almost-not-Wheeler.

I interpreted them as people who 3125 has already infected and "killed" by sucking everything out of them. Essentially, they are under its control, and while 3125 doesn't really have extremely malicious intents, its side effects are less desirable.

And of course, the more likely version is my being completely and irreversibly wrong, in which case you should go take amnestics. Or mnestics, so you don't tell people about what you heard so you don't confuse them. Or am I messing up my drugs again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amazing Series! Just wow…
But who/what are the not-people at the end supposed to be and what were they about to do to Wheeler?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I correct in assuming that SCP-3125 is connected to the contagious death of Bart Hughes from the first tale in the series?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3125 is a five-dimensional anomalous metastasized mass of bad memes

Sounds like my Facebook feed.

In all seriousness, I just found this via 2111, and binged it. This is possibly my favorite tale series on the site. Take that as you will considering I'm not the most versed in the intricacies of the SCP lore, but I absolutely loved this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cries tears of joy
I can honestly say that this is one of, if not my favorite tale series on this site. I have read the entire series five times now. Also, I'll be highly disappointed in you, qntm, if when the mainlist gets to the 3000s and 4000s, you don't post SCP-4739 and SCP-3125, although the latter not so much since it would only be the containment procedure unless you did a format screw where we see through the eyes of Wheeler.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap, this is amazing!

And the discussion about a team that are more capable at containment but less at intelligence makes me think of a simile that might help in combating these things.

Humanity is on the mnemonic level of a medieval warband fighting an aircraft carrier. The mnesiacs are their equivalent of a spyglass (Compared to the carrier's radar). It doesn't matter how hard they fight, their weapons and sensors and very being are just plain unsuitable to fighting an aircraft carrier.

Which is why they need to do a lot less fighting - it's absolutely pointless and they're utterly outclassed - and a lot more development of more powerful sensors and weapons, and - since we're discussing mnemonics instead of an aircraft carrier - more powerful (or rather, more adapted to the combat arena of mnemonics) beings.

How do you fight something you can't even conceptualize properly? You don't. You improve your ability to conceptualize, to remember, to not be influenced, until you can. Or create something that can do those things better than you can.
Stop spending resources fighting a specific threat and put everything into upgrading your own ability to fight!

item.title: ouch
item.content:encoded: Dying from an antimemetic bomb seems like an awful way to go. You'd be killed because you forgot your entire timeline of existence. Even if you remembered the need to breathe and eat instinctively, which you probably wouldn't because your instincts are information and all information in the blast radius is destroyed, you'd still starve because you wouldn't remember what food was or how to eat it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus, that was a great ending. But I forgot the part about 3125 finally descending on earth and possibly being dealt with. Will you help me remember by writing it at some point? Or are you just leaving it up to interpretation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Useless headcanon
- Useless headcanon

For whatever reason, I like thinking that SCP-1539 is actually the Ratio ex Machina (Hughes' machine). It makes no sense whatsoever given that it's in a laundry room and the machine is meant to be gigantic, but then again, some parts do fit a little too well – by "extracting" the concept of you, it's possible that the concept of "you" can do whatever it's meant to do, while your body just lies there in a meaningless mess provided nothing else enters the zone.

Presumably, 3125 stepped in and wrecked it, leading to its current state.

E: Useless headcanon aside, I feel Marion's fate may have been a little Muoka-y.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This must be my favorite series on this site. Definitely one of the best, too.

item.title: Re: Y-Class Mnestics
item.content:encoded:
I totally explained Y-Class mnestics but somehow you forgot all about it.

Seriously though, I don't know. Never quite figured out what would fit at that point on the sliding scale of mnestic intensity, and I didn't have a story for it. However:

  • It would do something different from W-, X- or Z-Class mnestics.
  • Its useful or beneficial effects would be offset by correspondingly nasty side-effects.

item.title: Y-Class Mnestics
item.content:encoded: You've explained W-, X-, and Z-Class, but what would Y-Class Mnestics do?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The idea of SCP-3125 being five-dimensional comes from two places, firstly, obsession with the number five"

I'm assuming that's why its SCP number is 5^5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pulpy high-action thriller fun dressed up in a brain-bending premise. I agree hearing the villain talk killed some of its mystery a bit… it was indeed a bit hammy and I'm not sure I'm super into the idea of what is essentially a Lovecraft monster sans the monster but… this is good. This is real good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this sooo much, as with the entire series. I just wanted to reply to this to say as someone who did stalk your sandbox after discovering the series I really loved reading the essay on mnestics, looping, memetics and anti-memetics. It was really fun to read and I think helped build the world. I'd say if/when an anti-memetics canon page gets created it would fit perfectly there or as a link as referenced internal foundation journal articles/training materials. Its too good not to put somewhere and it didn't seem like it would really trip anyone up in terms of limiting their writing, to me at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would be extremely easy to take this theoretical, abstract, high-concept subject matter too far, and end up with an unreadable mess.

You don't even come close to doing that. It's interesting and entertaining from start to finish. +1, as always.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gah, my brain …

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And with the AMD gone, MTF Omega-Zero is going to have a harder time communicating useful intel to the Foundation. Assuming that they even survived the events of this story…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to hypothesise that the two organisations were identical (they certainly both study the same collection of phenomena) but your idea for differentiating them is very smart for future stories. It means the CCD is more in the dark about exactly what is going on and what it's studying. It means it'll take them much longer than the AMD to put the pieces together and work out what SCP-3125 is (and of course, you can plausibly postpone that encounter indefinitely as a storyline if you didn't feel like going in that direction). And it means they stand a better chance of successfully containing or combating whatever they do encounter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In SCP-2111, I implied that Antimemetics was slightly better at intelligence and Counterconceptual is slightly better at countermeasures. That's why the former could read the writing, but the latter just knew it was dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoops! Keep forgetting SCP-2358 introduced the concept.

Anyways, thanks for the input!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's an interesting explanation. I guess I'll add it to the "Behind the scenes" file that we keep on articles and tales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: qntm might have a different take, but as the person who coined "counterconcept", they're basically the same thing with different names.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
qntm, I consider you the expert on stuff like this:

What's the difference between antimemetic and counterconceptual anomalies, assuming there is one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would be a very interesting addition to the article if it could somehow be implemented properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just read the -3125 entry… Hmm. You've got some really cool ideas there, but I agree that it'll take a bit of hammering out until it's got the right "fit" for the tone it looks like you're going for.

To address the inner/outer "boundary" - this is pretty interesting. If it were me, I'd go for something like a researcher note:

"To understand how dangerous -3125 is, one must first understand the conceptual space it exists in. Take the idea of an elephant: the ideas of 'gray', 'wrinkly', 'ivory' and so on are linked to it. The idea of pianos is linked to ivory, but not to elephants, and so you have to pass 'through' ivory to get from elephant to piano.

In a much more complicated manner, all ideas that can exist can be mapped into a sort of topographical map, representing how connected or distantly linked concepts are relative to each other. If all human ideas and knowledge are represented on that map, then -3125 represents a huge, black ink spill in the middle that has been marked 'Here There Be Dragons'.

What -3125 actually is, if it is anything at all, is obscured by the 'ink', which in this case is a tangled and warped web of ideas that have accreted around it. This may not sound dangerous to a non-memeticist, but I assure you that it is. Ideas can be dangerous, they shape our minds and our experience of the world. This shell of twisted ideas around -3125 is basically what Lovecraft was trying to get at when he wrote about how one could not observe an Elder God without being driven to madness: the ideas that it represents are so alien, so foreign to our experience that the mind is shattered and re-formed around it, nothing human left inside.

Of course, that 'ink' is just the concepts that one would have to pass through to even reach the ideas that -3125 itself is composed of, something so isolated from the rest of the map that it exists at the bottom of an informational black hole… But of course it's still just an idea. If one could somehow survive crossing the interface between the rest of the map and -3125's territory and catch a glimpse of what's in there, we could fight it. Create connections that didn't exist before, pull it out into the light, tear away everything that makes it dangerous and re-map the connections so it could never exist again.

The only problem with that is that it would be analogous to strolling down a black hole, pulling out the singularity and picking all the extra mass out until it fits into the universe properly again. If it wasn't for the fact that it breaks every physical and common sense law there is, it's not a bad idea."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's a Wikipedia article that should answer at least the second part.

Based off of some of the concepts in the linked article, if we treat the universe as nothing but a bunch of complex, multi-dimensional "information", then "memetic radiation" would be what happens when you screw around with that information on a fundamental, really-bad-idea-don't-do-that level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, it's a slight abuse of the terminology, yeah. But on the other hand I feel as if I'm kind of exploring a new science here which means I get to make up some of the rules.

Consider a physical object which people find anomalously difficult to remember. The idea of the object is an antimeme. Now, what could cause this effect? What mechanism? Could it be an intrinsic property of the object itself? Is it literally just an advanced form of optical illusion? If we made an atomic-level copy of the object, would this copy have the same properties?

Or is there something else at work here? Does the object have some kind of extrinsic, non-physical, anomalous property? Could this property have been bestowed upon the object somehow, and if so, how? A curse of some kind, which is attached to the idea of the object and spreads to wherever the idea spreads?

Here you can kind of see the analogy with radiation coming in. Suppose the object had been irradiated somehow. Then anybody observing the object would see it, but they would also get a dose of radiation at the same time. The radiation would affect them and make them ill, maybe. Now… suppose one of the side-effects of the radiation was forgetting about the object. Maybe in this case the "radiation" doesn't travel through physical space, maybe the "irradiated" property is attached to the idea of the object rather than the object itself, and the dose you get is what happens when some of the idea is transferred into your mind.

This idea of harmful, idea-destroying radiation extends fairly naturally to the idea of antimemetic "bombs" - which would be targeted not on a specific location but on a specific idea, and then effectively eradicate that idea (and numerous "bystander" ideas) by making the idea impossible to acquire anymore.

Of course there are many other ways to get antimemes… No reason why this should be the last word.

The idea of SCP-3125 being five-dimensional comes from two places, firstly, obsession with the number five (e.g. the Fifth Church, which is retroactively one of its manifestations). Secondly, part of the cosmology I worked out (which Marion refers to in We Need To Talk About Fifty-Five) is that there are two universes - the concrete one, made up of physical objects, and the abstract one, which is made up of ideas. I reckon if you take all possible ideas you can sort them into order according to all kinds of metrics like "complexity" and "abstractness" and "contagiousness" and "corruptibility". If you take all of those metrics together, you end up plotting an n-dimensional space, with ideas occupying points or regions in it. (I guess idea space is probably actually infinite-dimensional, and the facts are much more complicated; this is what (anti)memetic science is all about studying.)

Going further, I figure there could be a sort of ecology in that space - you can imagine ideas being eradicated, falling out of popular favour and being replaced by other ideas. This gives rise to the notion of SCP-3125, which is a sort of prionic infection in this space, which latches on to good ideas and turns them rotten, then spreads… and more importantly has some hostile boundary layer which prevents the idea of SCP-3125 itself becoming known. At the time of writing I have a draft of SCP-3125 in my sandbox which may also kind of clear this up (if it doesn't, let me know, it's just a draft and it'll be about six months before I can submit it in any case).

All of this is a little ad-hoc and I don't have 100% of the details ironed out but that's roughly how it works in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great as always, qntm.

In fact, the only thing that bothers me here is the "antimemetic radiation". To quote this guide:

An artifact can no more have a memetic aura or project a memetic beam than a creature could have a genetic aura or genetic beam.

It only seems logical that antimemetic effects should not spread through radiation either.

On second thought, there's another thing I'm starting to doubt here. How can 3125 be five-dimensional? it's essentially a bunch of information, so how can information have dimensions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hughes was killed some time after the bunker had been built but before he was able to begin work on the machine proper.

In my mind, the bomb came to be placed there by some separate chain of events, which may still have involved Hughes, but he certainly didn't do something as absent-minded as build the wrong machine by accident. The fact that the bomb is there at all shouldn't be entirely surprising, as it was revealed in the earlier Tale, Unforgettable, That's What You Are. The bombs aren't especially rare devices. Both the Antimemetics Division and their predecessor organisation, the Unthinkables, built and used numerous antimemetic bombs. As well as using them to combat dangerously contagious memes, each Antimemetics Division site had a bomb in the basement for use in emergencies, equivalent to the site nuclear warhead on other Foundation sites.

Of course, detonating the bomb eliminates all memory of the site. Over the course of the war, all of the other bombs had been detonated, which is why by the time of that Tale the Division has only one site left; in fact, the Division appears to have always only ever had a single site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely LOVE your Antimemetics stuff, qntm. I have a question, though.
Why, exactly, was the wrong device there? What drove Hughes or whoever put that bomb there to do that instead of building a machine to fight 3125? The Antimemetics Division should have been able to find a way to deal with such a dilemma, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the best summer blockbuster. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That'd be clever if you didn't already identify the worms in the story as the antimemetically cloaked animal equivalent of bacteria.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh yeah, I almost forgot! SCP-7381 is codenamed "The Vermifier". It doesn't disintegrate things, it converts them into antimemetically cloaked worms. The reason there are some on the floor in the freight elevator (and in Wheeler's hair) is that some of them fell on her when she shot the guy upstairs. She doesn't see them until the class-Z mnestics take effect.

When not-Kim vaporises those two airlock doors? Same deal. Worms, everywhere.

And thank you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Miéville. You went completely Miéville. Well, not completely, but from SCP-7381 to the half corpse leaving reality to the unresolved resolution to the alien state of the world … That's the author I see.

This is an incredibly well done story. Your work is impeccable, qntm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy hell. That is certainly an interesting concept that you could've added…

But I personally think that this article is perfect on its own. The buildup is suspenseful, the dialogue is very realistic, and it leaves us with no clue as to what happens next (in a good way though).

So, as I said previously, I'm extremely excited to see which direction you decide to take for your next article regarding antimemetic stuff (assuming you will).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My theory is that since she was exposed and then took the class Z, this is 3125 running on her own brain but unable to disable it, the end result being that it is temporarily borrowing sapience by making her schizophrenic. It is somewhat hammy villain dialog, because that's what she expects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a big aspect to this which I very nearly added to this whole story, but eventually shied away from. I was going to make it so that the huge invisible spider thing in "Unforgettable" was actually being ridden by a humanoid rider. When Wheeler shot at it, she hit the rider a couple of times. In "CASE COLOURLESS GREEN" I was going to have this rider come back and talk to Wheeler, directly antagonising her in much the same way that SCP-3125 apparently does in this chapter.

What would then happen is that Wheeler would deduce that this rider person, which claims to be SCP-3125, isn't SCP-3125 at all, but just another manifestation. In this case, some bratty kid which randomly encountered SCP-3125 somehow, and surrendered themself to it in exchange for a boatload of power. The kid thinks they're SCP-3125, and they do wield a great deal of power like free access to this (non-sentient) spider monster, and they think they're leading the war, but the kid is actually like a cuddly mascot strapped to the front of an eighteen-wheeler.

The lesson would be that no, SCP-3125 is not sentient or intelligent and does not communicate. It is just a massive primal force/prionic infection which has apparently sentient side-effects, and, in some cases, worshippers/servants. To some extent, it may even be that the SCP-3125's "If you see me, I will kill you" behaviour is just how this kid (and others) have chosen to defend SCP-3125 from attack.

So you've got three options there:

  1. Marion was really talking to SCP-3125, and it was giving her traditional villain backtalk (sorry about that, guess I need to work harder).
  2. Marion was talking to a "representative" which believes itself to be SCP-3125 but is actually just another hapless victim of this whole mess; a bratty kid with terrible dialogue and too much power.
  3. Marion was, as she suspects, genuinely just hallucinating.

Obviously there's no way you could work out the second possibility there, but that's on me, not you, so it remains a fair criticism.

This whole angle made its way into about half of the 30 or so drafts of "CASE COLOURLESS GREEN" but in the end I went in a different direction because it was making the entire story too complex. Although it's easy for me to explain all of this here in a forum post, it's relatively difficult to reveal such complex details in a fun, organic way through the medium of the story without resorting to infodump (and stretching it out past the end of 2015).

Thanks for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For added verisimilitude, there should be several asynchronous research guides posted, by different people, loosely at the same time…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job. My only critique would be that the section where she hears 3125 talk. That seems far too… traditional villain talk to me? I mean, I could be misinterpreting some brilliant twist (would be far from the first time) but assuming it's meant to be taken at face value it kind of downplays the incomprehensibility of it. That's the only real gripe I can think of right now. Upvoted enthusiastically.

item.title: *slow clap intensifies*
item.content:encoded:
Oh my god yes.
That was amazing. And, the story's open-ended nature makes this a great starting point for other people who might want to write about 3125.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Antimemetics Division is by far the greatest idea to come out of this wiki in the last several years, and this tale only reinforces that. You've put a hell of a lot of work into this series, and I'll be damned if it's going to be forgotten as easily as its namesake.
